petitioner_background: The petitioner is a mixed martial arts (MMA) fighter seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. He filed his petition in July 2013 and claims to train at an MMA academy with other professional fighters. The Texas Service Center Director initially denied the petition, the AAO dismissed his appeal on October 29, 2014, and the matter came before the AAO on a motion to reopen.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted that his 2013 wins at the Championships constituted qualifying awards. He also pointed to wins at 2012 events, including a match at the 2012 event and the 2012 event (which had eight winners, one from each match). He submitted an uncertified English translation of documentation regarding the 2012 event, and pointed to reporting on a website (covering MMA events) as evidence that the events were nationally/internationally recognized. He submitted a May 14, 2007 article showing a cross-promotional and content integration agreement between two websites, and asserted that the promotion was one of the most well-known MMA promotions.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence of participation in the 2013 Championships; the record only showed 2012 participation. The petitioner failed to show that the 2012 wins were nationally or internationally recognized. The 2007 cross-promotional article did not show that the partner website only covers nationally/internationally recognized MMA events — in fact, the article stated the site provides news about MMA generally, contradicting the petitioner's assertion that it covers 'solely nationally and internationally recognized fights.' The petitioner admitted the site 'does in fact cover a large array of fights and competitions.' Self-promotional material from the publisher cannot establish the publication's status (citing Braga v. Poulos). The uncertified English translation for the 2012 event had no evidentiary weight under 8 C.F.R. 103.2(b)(3), and in the alternative, it only showed the petitioner was one of 13 people who each won a match — with no evidence that winners received any prize or award. The 2012 event similarly had eight winners with no evidence of prizes or awards for winning. Finally, participation in events postdating the July 2013 filing date cannot be considered, as USCIS cannot consider facts that arose after petition filing.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted multiple articles from various Internet websites and Internet blogs, including materials from radarnoticias.com (described as 'a nationally recognized news site in Brazil, with over one thousand unique visitors every day'), a profile posted on another website, a printout titled 'Statistics' from a video website, and two video links to fights in which the petitioner purportedly participated.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that radarnoticias.com did not constitute professional/major trade publication or major media — its website traffic was ranked in an unremarkable position worldwide and its Google page rank was 0. The petitioner did not submit certified English translations, so the uncertified translations had no evidentiary weight under 8 C.F.R. 103.2(b)(3). Additionally, the materials were not about the petitioner relating to his work — they described the 2012 Championship and merely listed him as an event participant; the focus was the event, not the petitioner. The second website's self-promotional materials had limited evidentiary value per Braga, and the petitioner's profile lacked author and publication date information as required by the criterion. Its materials merely listed the petitioner as an MMA event participant who won a match, making them about events rather than the petitioner's work. The video website statistics related to the site as a whole (where anyone with internet access can upload videos with dramatically varying viewership), and the two fight video links contained no additional information about the petitioner or his work, and therefore did not constitute 'published material about [the petitioner]... relating to [his] work.'

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted he is a member of an MMA academy/team and trains with many other MMA fighters, including a named professional MMA fighter. He submitted reference letters from the Chief Executive Officers and Presidents of the academy, and a letter from a professional MMA fighter stating the petitioner is 'a huge part to the professional fight team.' He asserted he is 'an asset to the facility' and that 'training is critical for an MMA fighter.' No additional evidence was submitted on motion for this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to establish either a leading or critical role. Being an 'asset' to the facility, without evidence of a leading or critical role, is insufficient under the plain language of the criterion. The reference letters from the CEOs and Presidents did not discuss the petitioner's role in the fighting team or explain how his role is leading or critical. Evidence that he is a skilled and experienced MMA fighter, without more, is insufficient. The professional fighter's letter was conclusory, providing no basis for the 'huge part' statement — conclusory assertions without supporting documentation are insufficient (Matter of Soffici; 1756, Inc. v. Attorney General). Training with other MMA fighters does not constitute a role in an establishment or organization — the petitioner must show his place in the academy's hierarchy or his impact on the academy. Additionally, the evidence failed to show the academy or its fighting team had a distinguished reputation, because the reputation evidence came from individuals associated with the training facilities and team (self-promotional evidence with limited value per Braga). Finally, the AAO had previously questioned the credibility of two reference letters containing identical or virtually the same language, suggesting the language was not the authors' own (citing Surinder Singh and Mei Chai Ye), and the petitioner did not address these credibility concerns on motion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the motion to reopen on a procedural ground: the petitioner failed to submit a statement indicating whether the validity of the October 29, 2014 unfavorable decision was the subject of any judicial proceeding, as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii)(C), and a motion not meeting applicable requirements 'shall be dismissed' under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(4). In the alternative, even if the motion to reopen were granted based on new evidence, the AAO would reaffirm its prior decision because the petitioner failed to establish eligibility for the extraordinary ability classification by a preponderance of the evidence (Matter of Chawathe). The petitioner did not meet any of the three criteria he claimed (awards, published material, leading/critical role). Had the petitioner also intended a motion to reconsider, it would also be dismissed because he stated no valid reason for reconsideration supported by pertinent legal precedent or authority establishing that the prior decision misapplied law or USCIS policy. The burden of proof remains with the petitioner and has not been met.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
Braga v. Poulos, No. CV 06 5105 SJO, 2007 WL 9229758 (C.D. CA July 6, 2007), aff'd, 2009 WL 604888 (9th Cir. 2009)
Matter of Wing's Tea House, 16 I&N Dec. 158 (Reg'l Comm'r 1977)
Matter of Izummi, 22 I&N Dec. 169 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998)
1756, Inc. v. The Attorney General of the United States, 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.C. Dist. 1990)
Surinder Singh v. Board of Immigration Appeals, 438 F.3d 145 (2d Cir. 2006)
Mei Chai Ye v. United States Dep't of Justice, 489 F.3d 517 (2d Cir. 2007)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: (1) The motion was dismissed because the petitioner failed to include the required statement under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii)(C) about whether the unfavorable decision is the subject of any judicial proceeding. (2) The petitioner submitted uncertified English translations that failed to meet the regulatory requirements of 8 C.F.R. 103.2(b)(3), rendering them without evidentiary weight. (3) Credibility concerns: the AAO previously questioned the credibility and reliability of two reference letters because they contained identical or virtually identical language when describing the petitioner's achievements and abilities, suggesting the language was not the authors' own — citing Surinder Singh and Mei Chai Ye regarding inferences that strikingly similar affidavits indicate a common source. The petitioner did not address these credibility concerns on motion.
